journal_entry.description: Discounting of 90-day non-interest-bearing promissory note at bank (50 days after issue, 15% annual discount rate)
journal_entry.rationale: Ford discounts a $100,000 note receivable at the bank after holding it for 50 days. The bank charges a discount on the remaining 40 days (90-50=40 days) at 15% annual rate. Discount = $100,000 × 15% × (40/365) = $1,643.84. Net proceeds = $100,000 - $1,643.84 = $98,356.16. The discount charge represents a financing cost (expense) for early conversion to cash. Debit Cash for proceeds received ($98,356.16), debit Financing Expense for the discount charge ($1,643.84), and credit Notes Receivable to remove the asset ($100,000). This matches the tuple structure: 1 asset increase (cash), 1 expense increase (financing cost), and 1 asset decrease (notes receivable). No tax applies to financial instrument transactions.

output.reason: This transaction involves discounting a note receivable at the bank. On the debit side: (1) Cash = asset increase (receiving net proceeds from the bank), and (2) Discount/interest expense = expense increase (the discount charges represent the cost of early conversion to cash, which is the difference between face value and proceeds received). The note receivable will be credited (asset decrease) on the credit side.

debit_classifier.reason: This transaction involves discounting a note receivable at the bank. On the debit side: (1) Cash = asset increase (receiving net proceeds from the bank), and (2) Discount/interest expense = expense increase (the discount charges represent the cost of early conversion to cash, which is the difference between face value and proceeds received). The note receivable will be credited (asset decrease) on the credit side.

output.reason: This transaction involves Ford discounting (selling) a note receivable to the bank. On the credit side: (1) The note receivable (an asset) is removed from Ford's books = asset decrease. The bank calculates a discount for the remaining 40 days (90-50=40 days) at 15% annual rate, and Ford receives net cash proceeds. The difference between the face value and proceeds represents a loss on discounting, but losses are recorded on the debit side. The credit side only shows the note receivable being eliminated.

credit_classifier.reason: This transaction involves Ford discounting (selling) a note receivable to the bank. On the credit side: (1) The note receivable (an asset) is removed from Ford's books = asset decrease. The bank calculates a discount for the remaining 40 days (90-50=40 days) at 15% annual rate, and Ford receives net cash proceeds. The difference between the face value and proceeds represents a loss on discounting, but losses are recorded on the debit side. The credit side only shows the note receivable being eliminated.

output.reason: Entry correctly records the discounting of a note receivable. Balance verified: debits ($98,356.16 + $1,643.84 = $100,000) equal credits ($100,000). Accounts are appropriate: Cash (asset increase), Financing Expense (expense increase for discount charges), and Notes Receivable (asset decrease). Amounts are mathematically correct: discount calculation of $100,000 × 15% × (40/365) = $1,643.84 is accurate, and net proceeds of $98,356.16 properly computed. All necessary lines present. Debits and credits on correct sides for each account type. No tax applies to financial instrument transactions under IFRS.
